stem: A hospital uses an anti-HCV screening test to screen for hepatitis C. The test has a sensitivity of 96% and a specificity of 83%. The prevalence of hepatitis C in the patient population is estimated to be between 1.0% and 2.2%, with uncertainty due to regional variation and demographic differences in the hospital's catchment area. A patient receives a positive test result. What is the probability that the patient actually has hepatitis C? Provide your answer as a probability with a confidence interval reflecting the uncertainty in prevalence.

ground_truth.answer: P(hepatitis C | positive test) ranges from 0.054 to 0.113 as prevalence varies from 1.0% to 2.2%. Point estimate at 1.6% prevalence: 0.0841.

solution_steps: Apply Bayes' theorem: P(D|+) = P(+|D)×P(D) / [P(+|D)×P(D) + P(+|¬D)×P(¬D)], where P(+|¬D) = 1-specificity = 0.17.
The confidence interval [0.054, 0.113] reflects the range of posterior probabilities over plausible prevalence values.
common_failure_modes: Confusing sensitivity (96%) with positive predictive value
Providing only a point estimate without acknowledging the prevalence uncertainty
Ignoring the base rate (prevalence) and using the test sensitivity as the answer
